notes: Twelve stations, walked in order. Two notebook lines per station in the original; the 2048 remake auto-records everything. Categories (living/lost/line) are never displayed — they only shape the report register.

stations.name: the creek mouth
stations.arrive: 6:41 AM, the tide already turning. The creek is running backward with the last of the push, which it does four mornings a year and this is one. The county notebook is stiff with cold. Two ruled lines under STATION ONE, same as every station. The pencil is yours to lose.

obs.line: creek running inland, salt over sweet, approx. 40 yds
heron on the stump snag, hunting the wrong direction, catching things anyway
coil of blue poly rope, machine-cut end, no wear — lost, not discarded

stations.name: the first wrack
stations.arrive: The night's high line is drawn in kelp and everything the kelp caught. It is higher up the beach than the notebook's printed reference mark, which is the kind of fact the survey exists to hold.

obs.line: wrack line 2.5 ft above printed reference — highest of the notebook's examples
one gull working the wrack ahead of me, methodical, left to right like reading
doll's arm, porcelain, older than me, sea-frosted white
bull kelp holdfast still gripping its rock — brought the rock along

stations.name: the flat
stations.arrive: A hundred yards of mirror where the beach forgets to drain. Everything on it is doubled. Offshore, something surfaces once and goes down before the pencil is up.

obs.line: seal(?), one surfacing, 60 yds out, gone — logging it anyway
sand dollars, dozens, all face down — the flat buried its money
one wooden chair leg, turned, painted green twice, two different greens

stations.name: the midden bank
stations.arrive: The cut bank where the storms have opened the old shell midden. Whoever ate here ate here for two thousand years, and the tide is taking the evidence a spoonful a winter.

obs.line: midden exposure grown ~8 in. since last survey mark — the bank is giving it up
lens of pure mussel shell, thumb-deep, purple as the day of the meal
two crows auditing the fresh fall, unhurried, proprietary

stations.name: the drift log line
stations.arrive: The big logs, rearranged. The sea moves furniture nobody else can lift and never once gets credit. The gull from station two has kept pace the whole way.

obs.line: new arrival: old-growth butt log, 5 ft through, bark gone, one choker scar
same gull — confirmed, notch left wing — still working, still left to right
the schoolbus log has moved 30 yds north. it has been the reference since '98.

stations.name: the buoy reach
stations.arrive: The bell buoy is talking on the swell. The notebook, alone of all its lines, is pre-printed here: 'buoy, bell, tone (A3 · 220 Hz)'. Somebody at the county decided this one was always true. The notebook is right.

obs.line: buoy confirmed per printed line — tone steady on the swell, twice a wave
cormorants on the buoy rail, wings out, drying — the buoy's whole other job
glass float, softball size, green, net-scarred — twenty years crossing, one night arriving

stations.name: the outfall
stations.arrive: The old creamery outfall pipe, dry since the fifties, barnacled into geology. The seal surfaces again, closer, and this time it is certainly a seal and certainly watching the beach, not the water.

obs.line: seal again — closer, 30 yds, watching shoreward. watching me, if the line is honest
outfall pipe now fully reef — anemones the length of it, the county's accidental garden
one rubber boot, left, standing upright and full of sand like someone stepped out of it

stations.name: the cave mouth
stations.arrive: The sea cave the tide only opens at the lowest lows. This morning, after the highest high, it is exhaling — long cold breaths that smell like the underside of everything.

obs.line: cave venting on the ebb, ~8-second period — breathing with the swell
bat star at the entrance, vermilion, arm regrowing — the cave's doorkeeper
half an oar, the blade half, hand-shaped, old — the handle is somebody else's story

stations.name: the high berm
stations.arrive: The storm berm the king tide built overnight — gravel stacked like a decision. The gull is here first, standing on the crest, and for once it is not eating. It is looking at the same beach you are.

obs.line: new berm, 4 ft crest, full length of the reach — one night's work
the gull, notch wing, on the crest, not feeding — off duty, or on a different duty
jar, sealed, paper inside, ink gone to ghost — recording THAT it is, not what it says

stations.name: the graveyard of floats
stations.arrive: The back-eddy corner where the sea files its lost buoyancy: crab floats, net floats, one fender off something big. Every color that plastic ships in, sanded to pastel.

obs.line: float count 34 — up nine from the printed prior; the eddy is keeping more
winter wren hunting IN the float pile, threading it like brush — the sea's junk is habitat now
ship's fender, name band sanded to 'A—A'. recording the two letters that held on

stations.name: the shallows below the point
stations.arrive: And there is the seal. Ten yards, in the shallows, upright in the water the way they stand when the water is their chair. It is not hunting. The pencil is in your hand. It looks at the pencil, and then it looks at you, and the difference between those two looks is the widest thing on this beach.

obs.line: seal, 10 yds, stationary, upright — third sighting, certainly the same animal
new channel cut through the shallows bar — the point rearranged its doorstep overnight

special_watch.label: put the pencil down and watch
special_watch.text: You put the pencil down. The seal stays. The water breathes, the buoy says its one true thing far off, and for a while the survey has no surveyor — just two animals at the edge of the water, one of them off duty. However long it is, it ends when the seal decides, not you, which is how you know it counted. Both lines under STATION ELEVEN stay blank. They are the fullest lines in the book.

stations.name: the point
stations.arrive: The point, and the survey's end nail in the old fir, and the whole reach behind you in one look — creek to cave to berm to here. The notebook goes back in the bag. What it holds now is a coastline, the one you chose, two lines at a time.

remake.arrive_prefix: MERIDIAN HERITAGE · TIDELINE SURVEY · station telemetry nominal.
remake.compliance_note: HERITAGE COMPLIANCE · original interaction preserved per license §4(c)
remake.watch_text: You put the pencil down. There is no pencil; the gesture is preserved anyway, per license. The seal stays. The sensors, for eleven seconds, record nothing that was not already recorded, and the product does not know what to do with that, and does nothing, beautifully. It is the only moment in the cart that Ines Rocha wrote, and it survives the way a doorway survives a remodel: load-bearing.
remake.report_body:
33 of 33 observations logged. Coverage 100%. Mean confidence 99.1%. Walk duration 3 m 12 s (efficiency: excellent).

Nothing was missed.

[something was missed.]

Thank you for surveying with Meridian Heritage. Your coastline has been archived and will be preserved, complete and identical, forever.

living.title: THE LIVING SHORE
living.coda: Read back in order, your notebook is an animal census with a beach attached — the heron hunting backward, the gull reading the wrack left to right, the seal's three approaches. The county wanted the line. You gave them everything that crosses it. Somebody at a desk this spring will look up from your two-line entries and out a window, and lose a whole minute.

lost.title: THE SHORE OF LOST THINGS
lost.coda: Read back in order, your notebook is a catalog of arrivals — the rope, the doll's arm, the float that crossed an ocean for twenty years to be on time this morning. The survey wanted the tide line. You recorded what the tide was FOR. Every lost thing you wrote down is now found in the one way that lasts: on paper, dated, in somebody's hand.

line.title: THE LINE ITSELF
line.coda: Read back in order, your notebook is the beach's own pulse sheet — the wrack above reference, the berm built in a night, the bank giving up its shells a spoonful a winter. This is the notebook the survey dreamed of when it printed two lines and hoped. The measurements will hold. In some archive, decades on, they will be the only honest instrument this coast had.

whole.title: THE WHOLE BEACH
whole.coda: Read back in order, your notebook refuses to specialize — a seal, then a berm, then a jar of ghost-ink letters, in whatever order the beach offered them. This is either poor survey discipline or the entire point of sending a person instead of an instrument, and the county, to its lasting credit, has never once said which.

reports.footer: Filed with the county. Read, later, by exactly one person, carefully.
